Task from the shadcn curriculum (POC level L5-streaming-status-utilities):

# L5 — Streaming Status Utilities: shimmer and scroll-fade

Evidence: Live verified

## What This POC Proves

How shadcn's two streaming/status CSS utilities — `shimmer` (for "Thinking…/Generating…" placeholders) and `scroll-fade-*` (for transcript edge fades) — are provided and wired. They are **utility classes from `@import "shadcn/tailwind.css"`** added to `src/index.css` by `init`, not registry components. `npx shadcn@latest add shimmer` and `add scroll-fade` 404 against the live registry (CLI evidence in `source/add-shimmer-404.txt` and `source/add-scroll-fade-404.txt`).

The central honesty pivot, live-verified: the shimmer token is ALWAYS on `AttachmentTitle` regardless of state. The title's className is byte-identical whether `state="done"` or `state="processing"`. The activation predicate is the PARENT element's `data-state`, not a diff on the title's className. This POC proves the structural contract via RTL and explicitly documents that the visual fade/animation is CSS that jsdom cannot evaluate.

## Real Boundary Crossed

RTL + jsdom — real React 19 reconciler on real DOM. Asserts the structural class-wiring and parent-`data-state` predicate from the installed `attachment.tsx`. Also reads `src/index.css` directly (via `node:fs`) to confirm the CSS import is present.

The CLI 404 step crosses the registry HTTP boundary (real `npx shadcn@latest add shimmer` against `https://ui.shadcn.com/r/styles/base-nova/shimmer.json`); its output is captured in `source/add-shimmer-404.txt` and `source/add-scroll-fade-404.txt` as non-test evidence.

## How to Run

```bash
# From the degree harness app root (_poc-app/)
cd shadcn/degrees/01-chat-components/_poc-app && npx vitest run poc/L5-streaming-status-utilities.test.tsx
```

Expected: 5 tests, 5 pass, exit 0. Runtime ~433 ms.

The CLI 404 evidence was captured separately during the POC build (not re-run by Vitest):

```bash
npx shadcn@latest add shimmer --yes 2>&1 | tee source/add-shimmer-404.txt
npx shadcn@latest add scroll-fade --yes 2>&1 | tee source/add-scroll-fade-404.txt
```

## Prerequisites and Credentials

None. The Vitest tests require only:

- `npm ci` (or `npm install`) to restore `node_modules`
- The installed `src/components/ui/attachment.tsx` (written by L0)
- `src/index.css` containing `@import "shadcn/tailwind.css"` (written by L0 `init`)

No API keys, no network calls at test time.

## Ports and Cleanup

No ports opened. No network traffic during Vitest. No cleanup required.

## Red→Green Summary

RED: A test asserted that `AttachmentTitle.className` includes the shimmer token when processing but NOT when done. This is false — `done.className === processing.className` (byte-identical). The assertion `expect(cls("done")).not.toBe(cls("processing"))` fails: both strings are equal.

GREEN: Assertions corrected to the real contract — both states carry both shimmer tokens in their className; the active/inert distinction is the parent `data-state`, not any difference in the title's className. The `scroll-fade-x` token on `AttachmentGroup` and the `@import "shadcn/tailwind.css"` in `src/index.css` are also confirmed. 5 tests pass.

# POC Intent

POC level: L5
POC name: Streaming Status Utilities — shimmer and scroll-fade
Concept introduced: `shimmer` and `scroll-fade-*` as CSS utility classes shipped by `@import "shadcn/tailwind.css"` (installed by `init`), NOT as registry components (`add shimmer`/`add scroll-fade` 404); the honesty pivot that the shimmer token is ALWAYS on `AttachmentTitle` (className is byte-identical across all five states); activation is the PARENT `data-state`; the jsdom structural boundary — visual fade/animation is NOT assertable in jsdom (Playwright L6 proves the rendered fade).
Prior concepts reused: `Attachment` 5-state lifecycle (L3); `AttachmentGroup` scroll-fade-x token (L3); `data-state` on the root element (L3); RTL + jsdom harness (L1–L4).
Live service boundary exercised: RTL + jsdom for the structural contract (className tokens + parent data-state + CSS import). CLI 404 step (registry HTTP, real `npx shadcn@latest add shimmer`) captured as evidence-only output in source/ (add-shimmer-404.txt, add-scroll-fade-404.txt), NOT a Vitest test case.
Real resources required: Installed `src/components/ui/attachment.tsx` and `src/index.css` (both written by L0). CLI 404 evidence was captured against the live registry HTTP. No API keys.
Expected learning: Shimmer / scroll-fade are CSS utilities, not registry components — `add shimmer` / `add scroll-fade` 404 against `…/base-nova/shimmer.json` and `…/base-nova/scroll-fade.json`. They ship via `@import "shadcn/tailwind.css"` added to `src/index.css` by `init`; the `shadcn` runtime package in `dependencies` provides this CSS. The KEY insight: `AttachmentTitle.className` is byte-identical in ALL five states — the group-data-[state=processing]/attachment:shimmer and group-data-[state=uploading]/attachment:shimmer tokens are ALWAYS on the span. The activation predicate is the PARENT element's `data-state`. Asserting the title GAINS or LOSES the shimmer class on state change is FALSE and will fail. The correct structural test is: both tokens are always present in className; `closest('[data-slot=attachment]').getAttribute('data-state')` determines which group-data variant applies. jsdom cannot evaluate the CSS — no Tailwind application, no animation, no computed gradient. The real rendered fade belongs to L6 (Playwright/Chromium).
What this POC must prove: (1) `AttachmentTitle.className` is byte-identical across `state="done"` and `state="processing"` — and both contain both shimmer tokens. (2) The activation predicate is the parent's `data-state`: `closest('[data-slot=attachment]')` returns `data-state="processing"` for the processing case and `"done"` for the done case; in all cases the title className is the same. (3) All five states (`idle`, `uploading`, `processing`, `error`, `done`) produce the same title className string. (4) `AttachmentGroup.className` contains `scroll-fade-x`. (5) `src/index.css` contains `@import "shadcn/tailwind.css"`. CLI 404 evidence: `add shimmer`/`add scroll-fade` output contains "not found. It may not exist", captured in source/ (evidence-only, not a Vitest case). Explicit non-claim: the visual shimmer pulse and scroll-fade gradient are CSS, NOT evaluated by jsdom; the rendered fade is covered by L6 Playwright.
What would count as cheating: Asserting that title.className differs between states; mocking `@base-ui/react`; importing from a local fixture; asserting computed CSS or animation state in jsdom; fabricating the CLI 404 output without actually running `npx shadcn@latest add shimmer` against the live registry.

# L-hitl-tool-approval: HITL Tool Approval POC

Demonstrates the Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) tool approval safety primitive in Mastra 1.x.

## What it proves

- A tool with `requireApproval: true` causes `agent.generate()` to **suspend** (not execute the tool) when called with `{ requireToolApproval: true }`.
- `finishReason === 'suspended'` signals the suspension state.
- `suspendPayload` contains the `toolCallId`, `toolName`, `args`, and a `resumeSchema`.
- `agent.approveToolCallGenerate({ runId, toolCallId })` resumes execution and the tool runs.
- `agent.declineToolCallGenerate({ runId, toolCallId })` skips the tool — the agent responds without executing it.

| RED | `tests/red-unsafe-expectation.test.ts` | FAILS — tool is suspended, not executed |
| GREEN | `tests/green-hitl-approval.test.ts` | PASSES — suspend → approve → tool runs |
| REGRESSION | (in green file) | PASSES — decline → tool NOT executed |


Task from the llm-gateway curriculum (POC level L2-quotas-rate-limits-spend-caps):

# L2 POC: Quotas (Token) + Spend Caps (USD) + RPM Rate Limits — Over-Budget → 429, No Upstream Call

**Evidence: Live verified (L2 — 2026-06-21, real Fireworks serverless)**

## What This POC Proves

Three independent budget enforcement types, all proven live:

1. **Token quota** (`tokenLimit`): a tenant with `tokenLimit: 5` gets 200 on the first call (which records 82 real tokens — far exceeding the cap, because metering is post-paid). The NEXT call is blocked with 429 `{error:{type:"quota_exceeded"}}`. The upstream call counter does NOT increment on the blocked path — proven by `getUpstreamCallCount()`. A second tenant with no cap returns 200 while the first is capped (isolation regression).

2. **Spend cap** (`usdLimitNano`): a tenant with `usdLimitNano: 1` (1 nano-USD) gets 200 on the first call. After that call commits cost, the NEXT call is blocked with 429 `{error:{type:"spend_cap_exceeded"}}`.

3. **RPM rate limit** (`rpmLimit`): a tenant with `rpmLimit: 1` gets 200 on the first request within the minute. A second request within the same minute returns 429 `{error:{type:"rate_limited"}}`. The upstream call counter does NOT increment on the blocked path.

4. **RED → GREEN**: with `GATEWAY_RED=1`, the over-cap assertion on the TOKEN quota test is inverted to expect 200 (wrong — the real response is 429) — exit 1, 2 pass, 1 fail. Without the flag, all three tests pass — exit 0.

## Architecture Note — Same Gateway, TDD Lenses

**The control plane is feature-complete from L0.** The four modules are byte-identical across all six POC `source/` trees. L2 activates and proves the budget enforcement slice. The code does not grow level-to-level; the evidence does.

## Domain

Three tests, each with a fresh in-memory store:

- **Token quota**: `{tokenLimit: 5}` tenant; first call 200 (82 tokens metered, exceeds cap); second call 429 with `quota_exceeded`; upstream call counter unchanged; free tenant returns 200.
- **Spend cap**: `{usdLimitNano: 1}` tenant; first call 200; second call 429 with `spend_cap_exceeded`.
- **RPM**: `{rpmLimit: 1}` tenant; first call 200; second call 429 with `rate_limited`; upstream counter unchanged.

## Honesty Notes (PLAN §7-E — REQUIRED)

These are design facts, not flaws to hide:

**Post-paid metering + cap overshoot**: The gateway can only check accumulated usage BEFORE a call (pre-call gate on committed data). The cap-crossing call COMPLETES; the NEXT is blocked. A single call can overshoot a cap by an unbounded amount because `max_tokens` is tenant-controlled. A cap bounds cumulative spend to approximately **(cap + one max-size call)**, not to the cap itself exactly.

Live example: the `tokenLimit: 5` tenant's first call returned 82 real tokens — 77 over the cap. The cap was exceeded in a single call; the second call was blocked.

**Concurrency race**: Caps are evaluated against COMMITTED usage across an `await` boundary, so N concurrent in-flight requests for a single tenant can collectively exceed a cap by up to (in-flight − 1). For strict enforcement, serialize per-tenant or use a reserve-then-commit pattern. The sequential L2 test does not exhibit this race — it is disclosed nonetheless.

**RPM is in-memory and per-instance**: The RPM sliding window is not persisted to SQLite. It resets on process restart. Behind a load balancer with K instances, the effective RPM limit is `rpmLimit × K` (each instance enforces independently). Token/$ caps read shared SQLite — they are durable and shared across restarts and instances. This asymmetry is intentional and must be disclosed.

**Streaming is not supported**: the gateway meters on a single terminal `usage` object. `stream: true` requests return 400.

## Prerequisites

- Node 24 (node:test, node:sqlite, global fetch)
- `FIREWORKS_API_KEY` — a valid `fw_...` key (read from environment)
- `GATEWAY_ADMIN_KEY` — optional; defaults to `admin-secret`
- Zero external npm dependencies

## How to Run

```bash
# GREEN (exit 0) — 3 pass:
FIREWORKS_API_KEY="$FIREWORKS_API_KEY" node --test source/quotas.test.mjs

# RED (exit 1) — 2 pass, 1 fail (token quota test):
GATEWAY_RED=1 FIREWORKS_API_KEY="$FIREWORKS_API_KEY" node --test source/quotas.test.mjs
```

See `commands.md` for the full reproduce sequence.

## Expected GREEN Output

```
L2 token-cap upstream calls total: 2
✔ L2 token quota: under cap -> 200; over cap -> 429 (no upstream call); second tenant unaffected (5492.651ms)
✔ L2 spend cap: accumulated cost over usd_limit_nano -> 429 (spend_cap_exceeded) (600.777958ms)
✔ L2 RPM: over rpm_limit within the minute -> 429 (no upstream call) (563.153ms)
ℹ tests 3
ℹ pass 3
ℹ fail 0
EXIT=0
```

## Real Artifacts

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Token quota test: first call | `200` |
| Token quota test: over-cap call | `429` `{error:{type:"quota_exceeded"}}` |
| Token quota test: upstream calls (total, including second tenant's) | `2` |
| Token quota test: upstream calls on the 429 path | `0` (counter unchanged) |
| Second tenant (no cap): | `200` |
| Spend cap test: first call | `200` |
| Spend cap test: over-cap call | `429` `{error:{type:"spend_cap_exceeded"}}` |
| RPM test: first call | `200` |
| RPM test: second-same-minute call | `429` `{error:{type:"rate_limited"}}` |
| RPM test: upstream calls on 429 path | `0` (counter unchanged) |

## Key Gotcha Learned Here

The `tokenLimit: 5` cap is crossed by the very first call (82 real tokens). This is not an error
— it is the post-paid post-call metering model working correctly. The cap does not prevent the
first call from using more than 5 tokens; it prevents the NEXT call from proceeding once the
accumulated total is over 5. See `surprises.md`.

Task from the firecrawl curriculum (POC level L2-crawl-to-brief):

# L2 — Async Whole-Domain Crawl + Markdown Brief

## What this proves

This POC live-verifies two capabilities:

**P-002 — Async crawl jobs must be polled**
`start_crawl()` returns only a job reference (`CrawlResponse` with `.id` + `.url`). There is NO data and NO terminal status in the start response. You MUST poll `get_crawl_status(crawl_id)` until `status` is `"completed"` (or `"failed"` / `"cancelled"`) before page content is available. The TDD RED encodes this as a genuine failing test: under `SENTINEL_MODE=1`, the test asserts the start response IS completed — which fails because it is not.

**P-MAP — URL discovery with /v2/map**
`fc.map(url, limit=N)` discovers URLs on a domain before (or alongside) crawling. Returns `MapData` with `.links: List[LinkResult]`, each with a `.url` attribute (and optional `.title` / `.description`).

**Aggregation contract**
After polling, the POC aggregates the 3 crawled pages into a structured markdown brief with:
- A title heading (`# Firecrawl Domain Brief — <url>`)
- Per-page sections (`## Page N (<sourceURL>)`) with a 400-char markdown excerpt
- A `## Sources` section listing all polled sourceURLs in job order

The regression test locks this provenance contract: every page's `source_url` must appear in the brief and in the Sources section.

## How to run

### Prerequisites

```bash
export FIRECRAWL_API_KEY=fc-...   # or load from secrets.local.env
cd source/
python3 -m venv .venv
.venv/bin/pip install firecrawl-py==4.28.2 pytest==8.3.5
```

### TDD RED (genuine fail — no extra paid call)

```bash
SENTINEL_MODE=1 .venv/bin/pytest test_crawl_to_brief.py -v
```

Expected: `1 FAILED` (`test_sentinel_start_response_is_not_synchronous`), 14 PASSED.
The SENTINEL test asserts `start_response.status == "completed"` — which fails because `CrawlResponse` has no `.status` field (returns `None`). This is the exact P-002 bug encoded as a test.

### TDD GREEN (all real assertions)

Wait ~60s after the RED run (rate limit: 3 req/min on free plan), then:

```bash
.venv/bin/pytest test_crawl_to_brief.py -v
```

Expected: `14 PASSED, 1 SKIPPED`.

## Credit cost

- Map: ~1 credit
- Crawl (limit=3): 3 credits (1 per page)
- Total per full run: ~4 credits

The module-scoped fixture ensures EXACTLY ONE map call and ONE crawl per test session. All 15 tests share the same fixture output — no duplicate API calls.

## Key SDK facts (firecrawl-py 4.28.2)

```python
from firecrawl import Firecrawl
fc = Firecrawl(api_key="fc-...")

# Start async crawl — returns CrawlResponse(id, url) ONLY
start = fc.start_crawl(url, limit=3, formats=["markdown"])
job_id = start.id  # UUID string

# Poll until terminal
import time
cj = None
for _ in range(60):
    time.sleep(2)
    cj = fc.get_crawl_status(job_id)
    if cj.status in {"completed", "failed", "cancelled"}:
        break

# Access pages
for doc in cj.data:  # List[Document]
    print(doc.markdown)          # str
    print(doc.metadata.source_url)  # snake_case, NOT sourceURL
    print(doc.metadata.status_code) # int

# Map URL discovery
map_data = fc.map(url, limit=10)
for link in map_data.links:   # List[LinkResult]
    print(link.url)
```

## Artifacts

- `source/test_crawl_to_brief.py` — test suite

# L9 — flue build --target node: Deployable Artifact

## What this proves

`flue build --target node` compiles a flue project (workflows + config) into a self-contained
`build/server.mjs` artifact. This POC proves:

1. **Build fails on broken imports** (exit code 1, UNRESOLVED_IMPORT error) — RED test
2. **Build succeeds on clean project** (exit code 0, `build/server.mjs` produced) — GREEN test
3. **The built artifact is bootable** (`node build/server.mjs` starts a Hono HTTP server on PORT)
4. **Workflow is HTTP-invocable on the built server** (`POST /workflows/echo-build?wait=result`)
5. **Run records are persisted and retrievable** (`GET /runs/:runId?meta`)
6. **CF build produces partial output locally** but requires a Cloudflare account for actual deploy

## Prerequisites

- Node.js >= 22.18 (22.19.0+ recommended per flue CLI)
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in environment
- Port **3590** free (dedicated to this POC)

## Install and run

```bash
cd source/
npm install
```

### Run the RED test (build failure on broken import)
```bash
node test/run-and-assert.mjs red
# Exits 0 = assertion passed (build correctly failed with exit 1)
```

### Run the GREEN test (build + boot + HTTP invoke)
```bash
node test/run-and-assert.mjs green
# Exits 0 = all assertions passed
```

### Manual build + boot
```bash
# Build
npx flue build --target node --output ./build
# Boot on port 3590
PORT=3590 node build/server.mjs &
# Invoke
curl 'http://127.0.0.1:3590/workflows/echo-build?wait=result' \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message": "hello"}'
# Stop
pkill -f "node build/server.mjs"
```

### Cloudflare build (partial — deploy blocked)
```bash
npx flue build --target cloudflare --output ./build-cf
# Exits non-zero with Rolldown "agents" import resolution error
# Some output IS produced in build-cf/source/ (index.js, wrangler.json, assets/)
# Live deploy requires: CF account + wrangler + Durable Object migrations config
```

Task from the centaur curriculum (POC level L5-failure-modes-and-observability):

# L5 — Failure Modes and Observability

Live verified against Centaur centaur-0.1.61, OrbStack K8s, 2026-06-22.

## What it proves

This POC demonstrates three complementary failure-mode and observability claims:

### 1. Terminal execution failure (session.execution_failed)

A deliberate `max_duration_ms=50` (and `idle_timeout_ms=50`, which must be <=max) forces the
`spawn_max_duration_failure` path in the runtime. The event type `session.execution_failed` is
emitted with `"reason": "max_duration_exceeded"` and persisted to Postgres. This is a genuine
**terminal failure** of the execution — not a validation error or sandbox configuration issue.

### 2. Deserialization 422 is NOT a terminal failure

A garbage `harness_type` (e.g. `"NOT_A_REAL_HARNESS"`) returns HTTP 422 at the deserialization
layer and creates **zero** `session_events` rows. It never reaches the execution runtime.
This distinguishes input validation failures from true terminal execution failures.

### 3. Iron-proxy placeholder env audit

The codex agent subprocess receives `OPENAI_API_KEY="OPENAI_API_KEY"` (name equals value —
a placeholder). Iron-proxy intercepts outgoing HTTP to `api.openai.com` and swaps in the real
key. General stdout (like `echo $OPENAI_API_KEY`) is not rewritten. A live codex session that
runs `echo "OPENAI_KEY_IS=$OPENAI_API_KEY"` produces `OPENAI_KEY_IS=OPENAI_API_KEY` in the
`commandExecution.aggregatedOutput` field — confirming the placeholder is what the agent sees.
Real keys are never written to committed artifacts.

### 4. Postgres durability

`session_events` rows persist after the sandbox pod's lifecycle ends (sandbox paused/gone).
The event trail for the terminal failure execution survives as the durable source of truth.

## Test results (7/7 pass)

```
[PASS] RED-1: healthy turn — session.execution_completed in Postgres, no execution_failed
[PASS] RED-2: garbage harness_type -> 422, zero session_events rows
[PASS] GREEN-1: forced terminal failure — session.execution_failed in Postgres (SSE+PG)
[PASS] GREEN-2: Postgres — session.execution_failed row persists after execution
[PASS] GREEN-3: env audit — OPENAI_API_KEY placeholder verified via live codex turn
[PASS] GREEN-4: durability — Postgres rows survive sandbox pod lifecycle
[PASS] REGRESSION: healthy baseline still completes (regression guard)
7/7 tests passed
```

## Key evidence IDs

- Healthy baseline: thread `au:l5-healthy-1782146428`, exe `exe_1b75b2ef136b49f0bd63ce7b918ca803`, event_id 417 `session.execution_completed`
- Terminal failure: thread `au:l5-fail-1782146428`, exe `exe_ffd8860bd6834f71a52d0e2fd806d3ae`, event_id 420 `session.execution_failed` (`reason: max_duration_exceeded`, `max_duration_ms=50`), 5 Postgres rows persist
- Env audit: thread `au:l5-audit-1782146428`, exe `exe_05e58f1e2c0b4543bf690eaa09bdd429`, event_id 433 `commandExecution.aggregatedOutput="OPENAI_KEY_IS=OPENAI_API_KEY\n"`

## How to run

```bash
cd centaur/degrees/01-overview/03-pocs/L5-failure-modes-and-observability
python3 source/test_l5.py
```

Requirements: Python 3.10+, `kubectl` configured for the centaur namespace (OrbStack context).
No external port-forward needed. No pip installs — stdlib only.

## Prerequisites

- Centaur deployed locally: `kubectl get pods -n centaur` shows api-rs + postgres running
- If cluster is down: `centaur/scripts/deploy-local.sh` redeploys; `centaur/scripts/smoke-local.sh` re-verifies
- Prior levels L0–L4 must be green (cluster dependency)

## Architecture note

Iron-proxy security model:
- The sandbox agent subprocess (codex harness) receives `OPENAI_API_KEY="OPENAI_API_KEY"` — a placeholder where name==value.
- The iron-proxy pod (`asbx-*-proxy-*`) carries the real key and rewrites HTTP requests to `api.openai.com` before they leave the cluster.
- The api-rs pod carries the real key at the infrastructure level (needed to manage proxy configuration).
- This layered design ensures agent-level code never directly handles real credentials; the proxy is the sole trust boundary for credential injection.

# POC: L-meta-tool-mcp — COMPOSIO_SEARCH_TOOLS Meta-Tool

**Status: GREEN** — all 3 tests pass against the live Composio service.

## What This Proves

The degree's existing coverage only showed `session.tools.get()` (fetch schemas for known tool slugs). This POC proves the **search** surface: given a natural-language use-case, Composio returns ranked tool definitions with schemas, guidance, and cached execution plans.

This is the same capability exposed as the `COMPOSIO_SEARCH_TOOLS` meta-tool inside a session (the tool an LLM calls when it needs to find which Composio tools to use next).

## Key API

```ts
import { Composio } from '@composio/core';

const composio = new Composio({ apiKey: process.env.COMPOSIO_API_KEY });
const session = await composio.create(userId);

// Search the catalog by use-case
const result = await session.search({ query: 'send slack message to a channel' });

// result.toolSchemas is keyed by slug
// result.results[0].primaryToolSlugs = ["SLACK_SEND_MESSAGE"]
// result.results[0].recommendedPlanSteps = [...]
```

## Tests (TDD)

| Test | Role | Status |
|---|---|---|
| RED: fictional slug XYZZY_FAKE_TOOL_99 never returned | Proves assertion path is live | `test.fails()` passes |
| GREEN: "send slack message" returns SLACK tools | Proves real search works | passes |
| REGRESSION: each toolSchemas entry has toolSlug + toolkit | Shape contract | passes |

## Files

- `source/meta-tool-mcp.test.ts` — test suite
- `red-output.txt` — captured RED run
- `green-output.txt` — captured GREEN run
- `evidence.md` — tool names/toolkits from live results
- `surprises.md` — exact API shapes (camelCase transform, never-empty search, planId)
- `commands.md` — pinned install/run commands

## Credential Required

`COMPOSIO_API_KEY` only. No per-user OAuth consent needed.
Search works on ANY session (no toolkit connection required to search the catalog).

Task from the agentcn curriculum (POC level L-eve-gateway-api-key):

# L-eve-gateway-api-key — Durable Vercel AI Gateway API-Key Path for Eve

## What this POC proves

The agentcn `eve/weather` recipe can be run end-to-end through the Vercel AI Gateway using the **durable** `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` (a `vck_` key) instead of the short-lived (~12h) `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN`. This closes the operational gap left by the L4 gateway-asymmetry spine: an agent built from the registry can stay running without re-running `eve link` every half-day.

The proof is a real red→green transition driven over HTTP against a live `eve start` server, the live Vercel AI Gateway, and live Open-Meteo:

- **RED** — `eve start` with `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` only (no gateway credential): the NDJSON stream emits `session.started` then `step.failed`/`turn.failed` with code `MODEL_CALL_FAILED`, `gatewayName GatewayAuthenticationError`, `statusCode 401`. The assertion driver exits 1.
- **GREEN** — `eve start` with `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` set: the model issues a real `get_weather(location:"Tokyo")` tool call, Open-Meteo returns live current data (`temperature_2m 19.6`), and the model produces a grounded answer. All six assertions A1–A6 pass; the terminal `session.waiting` event fires; exit 0.

## Real boundary crossed

- **Eve runtime** — `eve build` + `eve start` (HTTP NDJSON session API on `127.0.0.1`), eve `0.12.0`.
- **Vercel AI Gateway** — base `https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1`; the gateway is the auth boundary that the `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` unlocks.
- **Open-Meteo** — keyless geocoding + forecast APIs called by the recipe's `get_weather` tool (real weather, not mocked).

## The single recipe edit

The shipped `eve/weather` recipe pins `model: 'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5'` (DASH form), which `eve build` rejects for missing gateway context-window metadata (see the L4/L8 model-string gotcha). The **only** modification made for this POC is pinning the DOT form `model: 'anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5'`. The point of this POC is the credential path, not the model.

## How to run

```bash
# Load credentials first (operator):
set -a; . .agent-university/secrets.local.env; set +a

# From source/
./run.sh red    3791   # ANTHROPIC only → expect 401 GatewayAuthenticationError, assert exit 1
./run.sh green  3792   # AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY set → expect grounded weather answer, assert exit 0
```

`run.sh` starts `npx eve start` on the given port, waits for `/eve/v1/info` readiness, drives a session via `assert.mjs`, prints the assertion result and the tail of the eve server log, and kills the server on exit.

## Prerequisites

- Node.js 18+ with `npx` available
- Internet access (Vercel AI Gateway + Open-Meteo + npm)
- `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` — durable Vercel AI Gateway key (`vck_…`), required for the GREEN path
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` — present in both modes (the recipe model is an Anthropic model routed via the gateway)
- No `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN` — deliberately unset in both modes (`env -u VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN`)

## Ports / services

`eve start` binds a local HTTP server (3791 for RED, 3792 for GREEN by default). The server is killed on script exit (`trap … EXIT` plus an `lsof`-based port reap). No cloud resources are created.

## Cleanup

`run.sh` kills its `eve start` process on exit. `npm`/`npx` may install `eve` into `scratch-project/node_modules/` (gitignored). Remove with `rm -rf source/scratch-project/node_modules`. See cleanup.md.

# POC Intent

POC level: L-eve-gateway-api-key — Durable Vercel AI Gateway API-Key Path for Eve
POC name: Durable Vercel AI Gateway API-Key Path for Eve
Concept introduced: Running an installed agentcn Eve recipe through the Vercel AI Gateway with the durable `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` (a `vck_` key) instead of the ~12h `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN`. This closes the operational follow-up to the L4 gateway-asymmetry spine — a registry-installed agent can stay running without re-running `eve link`.
Prior concepts reused:
  - L4 gateway-asymmetry spine: the recipe model string routes through the Vercel AI Gateway, so a bare `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is not sufficient to run it.
  - L4's proven NDJSON session driver (`assert.mjs`) — drive an `eve start` server over HTTP, read the NDJSON event stream until a terminal event, assert on `session.started` / `actions.requested` / `action.result` / `message.completed` / `session.waiting`.
  - The model-string gotcha (L4/L8): the DASH form `anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5` is build-incompatible; the DOT form `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5` is required.
Live service boundary exercised:
  - `eve build` + `eve start` (HTTP NDJSON session API), eve 0.12.0
  - Vercel AI Gateway (base `https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1`) — the auth boundary the durable key unlocks
  - Open-Meteo geocoding + forecast APIs (keyless) — called by the recipe's `get_weather` tool for real weather data
Real resources required:
  - `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` (durable `vck_` Vercel AI Gateway key) — for the GREEN path
  - `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` — present in both RED and GREEN (the recipe model is an Anthropic model routed via the gateway)
  - Internet access to the gateway, Open-Meteo, and npm
Expected learning:
  - The durable `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` is a working substitute for `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN` for the same gateway model call.
  - The exact RED failure when no gateway credential is present: `MODEL_CALL_FAILED` / `GatewayAuthenticationError` / `statusCode 401`, with the message "AI Gateway received no credentials. Run `eve link` to populate `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN`, or set `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY`…".
  - The recipe ships `model: 'anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5'` (dash, build-incompatible); the ONLY edit is pinning the dot form `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5`.
  - The runtime model id reported by `session.started` is `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5` on eve `0.12.0` (the degree snapshot used `0.11.10`).
What this POC must prove:
  - With no gateway credential and only `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, the eve session fails at the model call with `GatewayAuthenticationError 401` and the driver exits 1.
  - With `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` set (and `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN` unset), the same session runs to completion: a real `get_weather(location:"Tokyo")` tool call, a live Open-Meteo result, and a weather-grounded answer.
  - All six assertions A1–A6 pass and the terminal `session.waiting` event is present; the driver exits 0.
What would count as cheating:
  - Mocking the Vercel AI Gateway or returning a canned model response instead of a real gateway call.
  - Mocking or stubbing the Open-Meteo weather call instead of fetching live data.
  - Leaving `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN` set during the GREEN run — that would prove the OIDC path, not the durable key path.
  - Asserting the answer text from a cached transcript rather than the live stream.
  - Editing anything in the recipe other than the model-string dash→dot pin.

Task from the agentcn curriculum (POC level L1-install-a-recipe-via-shadcn-cli):

# L1 — Install a Recipe via shadcn CLI

## What This POC Proves

The `npx shadcn@latest add <url> --yes` command can install an agentcn recipe directly from the live registry onto the local filesystem. With the three required preconditions (package.json, components.json with tsx:true, and tsconfig.json), the CLI exits 0, writes exactly three files, and adds the framework dependency to package.json — all without any credentials.

## The Real Boundary Crossed

- **Registry read**: shadcn CLI performs a live GET to `https://agentcn.vercel.app/r/eve/weather.json` to retrieve the recipe manifest.
- **File write**: The CLI materialises three files from the registry-item payload: `agent/agent.ts`, `agent/instructions.md`, `agent/tools/get_weather.ts`.
- **Dependency install**: npm installs `eve@^0.11.10` (or latest) into the scratch project.

## How to Run

```bash
# From source/
bash run-poc.sh
```

The script:
1. Resets scratch-project to the RED state (removes tsconfig.json and agent/ dir)
2. Runs `npx shadcn@latest add ... --yes` → asserts exit 1 + "Failed to load tsconfig.json"
3. Adds tsconfig.json (GREEN condition)
4. Re-runs `npx shadcn@latest add ... --yes` → asserts exit 0, 3 files, eve dep
5. Runs an extra assertion: nonexistent recipe URL → "was not found" (not a crash)

Both raw outputs are written to `../red-output.txt` and `../green-output.txt`.

## Prerequisites

- Node.js 18+ with npx available
- Internet access (registry fetch + npm install)
- No credentials required (keyless)

## Ports / Services

None. Read-only registry fetch + local npm install. No server started.

## Cleanup

The script installs `eve` into `scratch-project/node_modules/` during the GREEN run. These are removed by the script automatically (or run `rm -rf source/scratch-project/node_modules`). No services to stop.

# POC Intent

POC level: L1 — Core Primitive
POC name: Install a Recipe via shadcn CLI
Concept introduced: The shadcn CLI as the installation transport for agentcn recipes. The full install handshake: registry fetch → file materialisation → npm dependency injection.
Prior concepts reused: Registry manifest structure (L0 probe), per-recipe endpoint shape (GET /r/<fw>/<slug>.json → files[] array), .json suffix requirement.
Live service boundary exercised: `npx shadcn@latest add https://agentcn.vercel.app/r/eve/weather.json --yes` — the CLI fetches the live registry endpoint and writes files locally.
Real resources required: None beyond internet access. No credentials. No local service.
Expected learning:
  - The three preconditions for shadcn add to work: package.json + components.json (tsx:true) + tsconfig.json
  - The exact failure mode when tsconfig.json is missing ("Failed to load tsconfig.json")
  - The exact success output ("Created 3 files: agent/agent.ts, agent/instructions.md, agent/tools/get_weather.ts")
  - The eve version pinned by the install (^0.11.10 at 2026-06-21)
  - The model string inside the installed agent.ts ('anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5') — which requires a gateway credential to run, NOT a bare ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
What this POC must prove:
  - shadcn@latest add fetches from agentcn live registry (not a local mock)
  - Exit 0 when all preconditions are met
  - Exactly 3 files are written at the documented paths
  - 'eve' is added to package.json dependencies
  - Exit non-zero with "Failed to load tsconfig.json" when tsconfig.json is absent
  - Nonexistent recipe URL produces a "was not found" error message (not a crash)
What would count as cheating:
  - Installing from a local copy of the registry JSON instead of the live URL
  - Manually creating the 3 agent files instead of letting shadcn write them
  - Asserting file content from a cached copy rather than the live install output
  - Mocking the npm install step

Task from the firecrawl curriculum (POC level L1-spa-to-json):

# L1 — Schema-driven Structured Extraction (json format)

## What it proves

Firecrawl v2's `POST /v2/scrape` with `formats=[{type:'json', prompt, schema}]` returns a
schema-shaped dict at `doc.json` — **not** `doc.markdown`. This is the live-verified
v2 structured-extraction path.

Key claims proven against the real API:

1. `doc.json` is a dict shaped exactly like the supplied JSON schema (`{title, summary}`)
2. Pydantic `PageBrief(title, summary)` validates the returned object (both fields non-empty str)
3. `doc.json['title'] == 'Example Domain'` (deterministic on example.com)
4. `doc.json['summary']` contains `"documentation examples"` (coordinator-confirmed phrase)
5. `doc.metadata.model_dump()['credits_used'] == 5` (json format costs 5 credits, not 1)
6. `doc.metadata.model_dump()['status_code'] == 200`
7. Regression: `set(doc.json.keys()) == {'title', 'summary'}` — no silent markdown fallback

This also resolves the G-001 discrepancy: this is NOT the legacy v1
`FirecrawlApp(...).scrape(url, params={"format":"json"})` route. The v2 path is
`Firecrawl(...).scrape(url, formats=[{"type":"json",...}])`.

## Why example.com (not a "JS-heavy SPA")

The planning doc named an aspirational SPA target. No public SPA gives reproducible
extracted-field values across unattended runs — LLM extraction is non-deterministic
on complex pages. `example.com` has exactly one title and one describable purpose,
so the schema extraction result is stable. The capability proven (schema-driven
json extraction at `doc.json`) is identical regardless of target complexity.

## How to run

### Prerequisites

- Python 3.9+
- `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` in the environment (the v2 hosted API key)

### Setup

```bash
cd source/
python3 -m venv .venv
.venv/bin/pip install -r requirements.txt
```

### TDD RED (genuine fail — no extra paid call)

```bash
SENTINEL_MODE=1 .venv/bin/pytest -v
```

Expected: 1 failed (`test_json_summary_contains_known_content` — sentinel not in real summary),
6 passed. The fixture makes ONE real API call; the sentinel-mode test asserts a deliberately
wrong value against the real response.

### TDD GREEN (all pass)

```bash
.venv/bin/pytest -v
```

Expected: 7 passed.

## Credit cost

5 credits per run (1 json-format scrape, shared by all 7 tests via module-scoped fixture).

Task from the apple-container curriculum (POC level L1-core-primitive):

# L1 — Core Primitive: Container Lifecycle

POC level: L1-core-primitive
Target: apple/container — Linux Containers on macOS
Status: Complete with live evidence

## What this POC proves

A container can be created in detached mode, observed with `container ls` to verify
its NAT IP (192.168.64.0/24 subnet), exec-d into to confirm `EXEC_OK` and eth0 IP,
inspect-ed to verify id and image fields, logged (empty stream for sleep is correct),
stopped, removed, and verified absent — all against the live `container` daemon on
macOS 15 without root.

## Key outputs

- Container `au-l1-life` assigned NAT IP `192.168.64.29/24` (live run, IP varies per start)
- `container exec` confirms `EXEC_OK` and matching eth0 inet
- `container inspect` returns structured JSON with id, image, network, resource fields
- `container logs` exits 0 with empty stream (correct for `sleep`)
- Stop takes ~5 seconds; rm is immediate
- `container inspect` JSON escapes forward-slashes as `\/` — a grep gotcha

## TDD result

RED phase: 3/3 intentional failures confirmed.
GREEN phase: 10/10 assertions passed against real daemon.

## Files

- `source/test-lifecycle.sh` — runnable TDD bash test
- `red-output.txt` — captured RED run
- `green-output.txt` — captured GREEN run (raw daemon output)
- `intent.md`, `test-plan.md`, `evidence.md`, `status.md` — structured POC docs
- `implementation-notes.md`, `observability-notes.md`, `surprises.md`, `commands.md`, `cleanup.md`

# POC Intent

POC level: L1-core-primitive
POC name: Container Lifecycle
Concept introduced: Full container lifecycle — run (detached), ls, exec, inspect, logs, stop, rm
Prior concepts reused: Daemon running (L0), alpine:3.20 image already cached, PATH prefix pattern
Live service boundary exercised: apple/container daemon (container-apiserver via XPC, Virtualization.framework VMs)
Real resources required: Running container daemon (already started); alpine:3.20 image (already pulled by L0)
Expected learning:
  - How to start a container in detached mode and retrieve its ID
  - How to read the assigned NAT IP from `container ls`
  - How `container exec` crosses the VM boundary to run commands inside the guest
  - How `container inspect` returns structured JSON for configuration and network state
  - How `container logs` handles containers with no stdout (empty stream, exit 0)
  - How stop and rm work, and that stop has a measurable latency (~5s for sleep)
  - That inspect JSON uses escaped forward-slashes (\/), which breaks naive grep patterns
What this POC must prove:
  - A detached container acquires a 192.168.64.0/24 NAT IP automatically
  - `container exec` can run arbitrary commands in the guest and read the eth0 IP
  - The lifecycle commands (ls/inspect/logs/stop/rm) all succeed against the real daemon
  - The container is fully cleaned up after stop+rm
What would count as cheating:
  - Mocking the container CLI or daemon responses
  - Using a local stub instead of the real apple/container daemon
  - Not actually running a container VM
  - Pre-populating the IP or exec output without a real exec
Why cheating would destroy the learning:
  - The IP allocation, exec latency, inspect JSON shape, and stop timing only appear
    against a real VM-backed daemon; mocks would miss the JSON-slash-escaping gotcha,
    the ~5s stop time, and the exact `container ls` column layout

Task from the eve curriculum (POC level L5-human-in-the-loop-approval):

# L5 — Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) Approval

## What this POC proves

Eve's `needsApproval` tool property durably pauses a running session and emits an `input.requested`
stream event carrying the pending approval request. The caller answers via `inputResponses` in a
follow-up POST. When approved, the tool executes and the session resumes. When denied, the tool
does not execute. The pause is durable — the workflow suspends without holding compute.

## Feature exercised

- `needsApproval: always()` on a tool (from `eve/tools/approval`) — gating the
  `transfer_funds` tool on human approval before every call.
- `input.requested` stream event carrying `requestId`, `prompt`, `options` (`approve`/`deny`).
- Continuation token resume via `inputResponses` keyed by `requestId`.
- Approve path: `action.result` fires with completed transfer output.
- Deny path: no `action.result` with `status: "completed"` — tool did not execute.

## Deployed URL

`https://eve-poc-l5-approval-bnbv5y0mp-dennisons-projects.vercel.app`

`GET /eve/v1/health` — public, no auth needed.
Sessions require HTTP Basic auth (`operator:<ROUTE_AUTH_BASIC_PASSWORD>`).

## Prereqs

- Node 24+
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in env
- `ROUTE_AUTH_BASIC_PASSWORD` set (any value) for local basic-auth; empty string works locally

## Run locally

```bash
cd source/app
npm install
npx eve build
npx eve start --port 3015
```

In a second terminal:
```bash
node assert-hitl.mjs
# or with explicit empty password (local default):
ROUTE_AUTH_BASIC_PASSWORD= node assert-hitl.mjs
```

## Project structure

```
agent/
  agent.ts          — defineAgent with anthropic("claude-haiku-4-5")
  instructions.md   — system prompt
  tools/
    transfer_funds.ts — HITL-gated tool (needsApproval: always())
  channels/
    eve.ts          — route-auth: vercelOidc + httpBasic
assert-hitl.mjs     — RED→GREEN assertion script (full pause→approve→complete loop)
```

Task from the agentcn curriculum (POC level L7-config-surface-easy-customization):

# L7 — Config surface: make the agent easily customizable

## What this proves

This POC demonstrates the **config-surface pattern** — the "easy customization" centerpiece of the
agentcn degree. An installed production recipe is refactored so that ALL customization knobs
(model, persona/instructions, enabled tools, tool parameters) live in a single typed config file
(`agent.config.ts`) with ENV overrides. The core agent file (`agents/weather.ts`) is NEVER edited
to recustomize.

**Two config-only changes, no core-logic edits, two distinct live behaviors:**

1. **Persona flip** — set `AGENT_PERSONA` env → response style changes from friendly weather prose
   to terse nautical brevity codes (FAVORABLE/CAUTION/HAZARDOUS). `agents/weather.ts` unchanged.

2. **Tool disable** — set `AGENT_TOOLS=get_air_quality` → `get_weather` is dropped from the tool
   list; the agent correctly reports it cannot access temperature/weather data, and instead calls
   `get_air_quality` with real Open-Meteo AQI data (us_aqi=77, pm2_5=19.2 for Tokyo).

Both runs hit real Open-Meteo APIs (no mocks). Run IDs captured.

## Key files

- `source/proj/agent.config.ts` — the config surface (model + persona + enabledTools + toolParams + ENV overrides)
- `source/proj/agents/weather.ts` — core agent reads config; no customization logic here
- `source/proj/tools/get-weather.ts` — reconciled tool (input: + run(ctx))
- `source/proj/tools/get-air-quality.ts` — second tool, toggled via enabledTools

## Reconciliation note

The registry recipe (`flue/weather`) ships with the legacy API (`parameters:`, `execute:`,
`createAgent + route export`) which throws `ToolLegacyDefinitionError` on @flue/runtime beta.6.
Reconciling to `input:`, `run(ctx)`, and `defineAgent` is the first step — and itself a teachable
about owning installed source.

## How to reproduce

See `commands.md` for the exact reproducible sequence.

# Intent — L7: Config surface: make the agent easily customizable

## Axis
easy-customize (centerpiece POC for this axis)

## What we are proving
That a production recipe can be refactored to expose a typed config surface — a single
`agent.config.ts` file (plus ENV overrides) — so that an operator or downstream agent can
recustomize model, persona, enabled tools, and tool parameters WITHOUT touching the core
agent or tool implementation files.

## The pattern
```
agent.config.ts         ← EDIT HERE (or set ENV vars)
  model: string         ← AGENT_MODEL env override
  persona: string       ← AGENT_PERSONA env override
  enabledTools: string[] ← AGENT_TOOLS env override (comma-separated)
  toolParams: {}

agents/weather.ts       ← DO NOT EDIT (reads config, builds tool list dynamically)
tools/*.ts              ← DO NOT EDIT (implementations, registered in ALL_TOOLS map)
```

## Net-new vs other POCs
- L0 edits the agent file directly (swap model/persona)
- L7 introduces the CONFIG LAYER that eliminates the need to edit the agent file at all
- L8 repackages L7 as a redistributable registry item

## Flue specifics
- Framework: Flue (@flue/cli + @flue/runtime ^1.0.0-beta.6)
- Credentials: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY direct (no gateway)
- Tool registry: ALL_TOOLS map, filtered by enabledTools list
- ENV precedence: env var > config default
- Run mode: ephemeral (`npx @flue/cli@latest run weather --target node`)

A developer's attempt FAILS with:
```
# RED State — as-shipped recipe fails on current Flue API

Command:
  npx @flue/cli@latest run weather --target node --input '{"message":"What is the weather in Tokyo?"}'

Files: agents/weather.ts (createAgent + route export), tools/get-weather.ts (parameters: + execute:)
       — exactly as downloaded from https://www.agentcn.run/r/flue/weather.json

Exit code: 1

Error output:
  Error: Agent failed: This tool uses the unsupported legacy definition format.

Root cause:
  The registry ships tools using the OLD Flue API:
    - `parameters: v.object(...)` instead of `input: v.object(...)`
    - `execute: async ({ location }) => {...}` instead of `run: async (ctx) => { const {location} = ctx.input; ... }`
  The agent uses `createAgent(() => ({...}))` with `export const route: AgentRouteHandler`
  instead of `defineAgent(() => ({...}))`.

  On @flue/runtime ^1.0.0-beta.6, the runtime throws `ToolLegacyDefinitionError`
  (surfaced as "unsupported legacy definition format") and exits with code 1.

Lesson captured: "production-ready" ≠ "runs unmodified on latest" — the recipe is owned
source you must reconcile to the current framework version.

```

Task from the elysiajs curriculum (POC level L2-lifecycle-error-handling-guards):

# L2 POC: Request Lifecycle, Centralized Error Mapping & Guard-Based Auth

## What This POC Proves

1. **Custom error registration**: `.error({ NOT_FOUND: NotFoundError })` binds a custom `Error` subclass to the string code `'NOT_FOUND'`, making it dispatchable in `onError`.

2. **Centralized error mapping**: A single `.onError(({ code, error, status }) => ...)` handler intercepts both custom codes (`'NOT_FOUND'`) and Elysia's built-in codes (`'VALIDATION'`), mapping each to a clean HTTP response via `status(httpCode, body)`.

3. **Real HTTP status shapes**: Unknown user → `404 {"error":"user not found"}`; invalid body → `422 {"error":"validation","detail":[...]}` with a per-field TypeBox issue array.

4. **Guard-based auth**: `.guard({ headers: t.Object({ authorization: t.Optional(t.String()) }) })` + a `beforeHandle`-style inline check gives `/secret` 401 without a token and 200 with `Bearer sekret`.

5. **RED→GREEN exit-code transition**: The RED run asserts `200` on a known-absent user (real status is `404`), forcing `bun test` exit 1. The GREEN run corrects to `404` and adds the full regression suite; `bun test` exits 0.

6. **REGRESSION**: The same `onError` dispatches the built-in `VALIDATION` code (triggered by a body with `name: ''`, `age: -1`) to `422` with a `detail` array — one handler, two codes, two HTTP statuses.

## Concepts Reused from Prior Levels

- L0: `app.handle(new Request(...))` in-process seam — no port, no mocking.
- L1: TypeBox `t.Object({ name: t.String({ minLength: 1 }), age: t.Number({ minimum: 0 }) })` body validation that emits `VALIDATION` errors.

## Prerequisites

- Bun 1.3.14 (`~/.bun/bin/bun`)
- No API keys, no network egress, no port

## How to Run

```bash
cd elysiajs/degrees/01-type-safe-apis-on-bun/03-pocs/L2-lifecycle-error-handling-guards/source
export PATH="$HOME/.bun/bin:$PATH"
bun add elysia @elysiajs/eden @elysiajs/openapi   # install once; bun.lock is committed
bun test
```

Expected output (GREEN):

```
bun test v1.3.14 (0d9b296a)

 4 pass
 0 fail
 12 expect() calls
Ran 4 tests across 1 file. [60.00ms]
EXIT=0
```

## Real Response Bodies Captured (verbatim)

| Route | Status | Body |
|---|---|---|
| GET /users/9999 | 404 | `{"error":"user not found"}` |
| GET /secret (no token) | 401 | `{"error":"unauthorized"}` |
| GET /secret (Bearer sekret) | 200 | `{"ok":true}` |
| POST /users (invalid body) | 422 | `{"error":"validation","detail":[{"/name" issue},{"/age" issue}]}` |

The `detail` array is TypeBox's `error.all` — each element has `path`, `summary`, `type`, `schema`, `value`.

## Key Architecture Pattern

```
.error({ NOT_FOUND: NotFoundError })          // 1. register custom class
.onError(({ code, error, status }) => {       // 2. one handler, many codes
  if (code === 'NOT_FOUND') return status(404, { error: error.message })
  if (code === 'VALIDATION') return status(422, { error: 'validation', detail: error.all })
})
.get('/users/:id', ...) // throws NotFoundError -> caught above
.guard({ headers: t.Object({ authorization: t.Optional(t.String()) }) })
.get('/secret', ({ headers, status }) => {
```

# L1 POC: Core Primitive — Sessions and Branching

**Degree**: pi AI School — 01 Overview
**Level**: L1
**Status**: complete
**Date**: 2026-05-17

## Purpose

Prove two core primitives of the pi coding agent:

1. **Session persistence** (`B-L1-1`): When pi is invoked in print mode without `--no-session`, it writes a structured JSONL session file under `~/.pi/agent/sessions/`. The file is valid newline-delimited JSON containing the user prompt and assistant response.

2. **RPC mode driver** (`B-L1-2`): When pi is invoked with `--mode rpc`, a Node.js driver can send `{ "type": "prompt", "message": "..." }` commands over stdin and observe `agent_start`, `message_update`, and `agent_end` events on stdout as newline-delimited JSON.

## Live boundary

Ollama at `localhost:11434`, model `llama3.1:8b`. All tests require a live LLM round-trip — no mocking.

## Key discoveries

- Pi uses CWD-keyed subdirectories for sessions, not flat files at the sessions root.
- The slug algorithm is: `'-' + (realPath + '/').replace(/\//g, '-') + '-'`
- macOS symlinks (`/tmp` → `/private/tmp`, `/var` → `/private/var`) must be resolved before computing the slug.
- RPC mode emits a `response` event confirming command receipt before the `agent_start` event.
- The `agent_end` event carries the full `messages` array (user + assistant).

## Tests

| File | Behavior | Category |
|------|----------|----------|
| `tests/session-jsonl.test.ts` | B-L1-1: session JSONL created and valid | Live behavioral |
| `tests/rpc-driver.test.ts` | B-L1-2: RPC events fire in order | Live behavioral |
| `tests/regression.test.ts` | Sessions dir exists; JSONL parses | Local helper |

## Run

```bash
cd source
npm install
npm test
```

Requires Ollama running at `localhost:11434` with `llama3.1:8b` loaded.

# Intent — L1 Core Primitive: Sessions and Branching

## Behaviors under test

### B-L1-1: Session persistence via print mode

**Assertion**: When pi is invoked as `pi -p "<prompt>" --mode text --provider ollama --model llama3.1:8b --no-tools` (without `--no-session`), and the process exits cleanly, pi creates a JSONL session file at:

```
~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<timestamp>_<uuid>.jsonl
```

The file must:
- Be non-empty (more than one line)
- Parse as valid newline-delimited JSON on every line
- Contain a `{ "type": "session", ... }` header as the first entry
- Contain at least one `{ "type": "message", "message": { "role": "user", ... } }` entry referencing the prompt text
- Contain at least one `{ "type": "message", "message": { "role": "assistant", ... } }` entry with non-empty text content

### B-L1-2: RPC mode event stream

**Assertion**: When pi is invoked as `pi --mode rpc --provider ollama --model llama3.1:8b --no-tools --no-session`, a Node.js driver can:
1. Send `{ "type": "prompt", "message": "<text>" }\n` over stdin
2. Receive a `{ "type": "response", "command": "prompt", "success": true }` confirmation
3. Observe `agent_start` → `message_update` (one or more) → `agent_end` in the stdout event stream
4. Find the sentinel text in at least one `message_update` event
5. Close stdin and wait for pi to exit after `agent_end` fires

## Success criteria

- Both B-L1-1 and B-L1-2 behavioral tests pass with Ollama as the live provider
- All test output captured in `green-output.txt`
- Three TDD commits (red → green → regression) visible in git log

## Partial success criteria

- `partial`: One of the two behavioral tests passes; the other is blocked
- `blocked`: Neither test passes due to infrastructure or pi API issues

## What would constitute partial success

- If RPC mode (`--mode rpc`) is not available in pi 0.75.1, B-L1-2 would be blocked while B-L1-1 could still pass
- If session persistence is disabled by default in some pi configurations, B-L1-1 would be blocked while B-L1-2 could still pass

# POC: compat.forceAdaptiveThinking on Custom OpenAI-Compatible Endpoint

**Key**: `pi/force-adaptive-thinking`  
**Status**: partial  
**Pi version**: 0.78.0 (flag added in 0.75.5)  
**Date**: 2026-06-17  

## What this POC tests

Pi 0.75.5 added `compat.forceAdaptiveThinking` to the custom provider/model config schema.
This flag is designed to enable adaptive-thinking Claude behavior on custom providers without
requiring a change to pi's built-in model registry.

This POC verifies:
1. The flag is **accepted** by pi's schema validator for a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint
2. Pi **routes** to the custom endpoint (OpenRouter) with the flag set
3. The turn **completes** successfully (exit 0)

## Red / Green

### RED (baseline without flag)
- Custom provider `openrouter-custom-poc` pointing at OpenRouter's OpenAI-compat endpoint
- No `compat.forceAdaptiveThinking` in config
- Result: pi lists the model, runs the turn, returns sentinel, exits 0

### GREEN (with flag)
- Same provider + endpoint + model
- `compat.forceAdaptiveThinking: true` added at provider level
- Result: config accepted (no schema error), pi routes to custom endpoint, turn completes, exits 0

## Status: partial

The flag is schema-valid and accepted for any provider type (due to TypeBox Union). Pi routes
to the custom endpoint and completes the turn successfully with the flag present.

However, the **behavioral effect** of `forceAdaptiveThinking` (sending `thinking.type: "adaptive"`
+ `output_config.effort`) only fires in `pi-ai/dist/providers/anthropic.js` when the provider
uses `api: "anthropic-messages"`. On an `openai-completions` provider, the flag is accepted but
silently ignored by the adapter.

For **full adaptive-thinking behavior**: configure the custom provider with `api: "anthropic-messages"`
and a Claude model with `reasoning: true`. See `commands.md` for an example.

# L2: Subagents + Skills POC

## What this POC proves

This POC demonstrates two Eve features working together in a single agent:

1. **Declared subagent delegation** — the root agent delegates a focused subtask to a specialist `researcher` subagent declared at `agent/subagents/researcher/`. The parent stream emits `subagent.called` and `subagent.completed` control-plane events. The subagent runs in its own child session with fresh state and its own instructions.

2. **Load-on-demand skill** — after receiving the researcher's findings, the root agent loads the `citation-format` skill via the built-in `load_skill` tool. The skill injects its markdown procedure into the active turn's context, guiding the model to format the final answer in a consistent structure.

Together these prove the two lightweight composition primitives Eve offers: skills for optional procedures, subagents for specialist delegation.

## Features exercised

- `agent/subagents/researcher/agent.ts` — declared subagent with `description`
- `agent/subagents/researcher/instructions.md` — subagent-scoped instructions
- `agent/skills/citation-format.md` — flat markdown skill with `description` frontmatter
- `agent/channels/eve.ts` — route-auth with `localDev()` + `vercelOidc()` + `httpBasic()`
- NDJSON stream events: `subagent.called`, `subagent.completed`, `action.result` (load_skill)

## Deployed URL

`https://eve-poc-l2-subagents-pyyrz386e-dennisons-projects.vercel.app`

- `GET /eve/v1/health` — public, 200
- `POST /eve/v1/session` — requires `Authorization: Basic operator:<ROUTE_AUTH_BASIC_PASSWORD>`

## Prerequisites

- Node 24+
- `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` exported

## Run locally

```bash
cd source/app
npm install
npx eve dev --no-ui --port 3012   # dev auto-installs microsandbox
# in another terminal:
node assert-l2.mjs                 # no auth needed locally (localDev() passes)
```

## Notes

- `npx eve start --port 3012` fails locally because `microsandbox` is not installed. Use `npx eve dev --no-ui --port 3012` instead — it auto-installs the optional dep.
- The `load_skill` tool fails with `SandboxTemplateNotProvisionedError` in the Vercel deployment (skills require a provisioned sandbox template). The model gracefully falls through and still delivers the subagent's findings. See `surprises.md`.
- Locally, `load_skill` succeeds and the model follows the citation-format procedure exactly (formatting with "Key Facts" and "Source: researcher subagent").

Task from the sqlite curriculum (POC level L16-custom-functions-collations-aggregates):

# L16 — Custom Functions, Collations, and Aggregates

## What this POC teaches

How to extend SQLite from the host language using three mechanisms:

1. **Scalar UDF** (`db.function`) — register a JavaScript function callable from SQL (e.g. `rev('abc')` returns `'cba'`).
2. **Custom aggregate** (`db.aggregate`) — register a JS accumulator with `{start, step, result}` phases, callable as a SQL aggregate function (e.g. `sum_sq(n)` returns the sum of squares).
3. **Natural-sort ordering** — demonstrate that SQLite's BINARY collation gives wrong lexicographic order for strings like `item2` vs `item10`, then fix it with a scalar UDF used in `ORDER BY`.

**Key driver gap discovered live:** Neither `node:sqlite` nor `better-sqlite3` exposes `sqlite3_create_collation`. True custom collations (as a `COLLATE <name>` clause) require either a C extension loaded via `db.loadExtension()` or a binding that wraps the C API. The ordering behavior is fully reproduced via a `nat_key()` scalar UDF in `ORDER BY`. See `implementation-notes.md` for details.

## How to run

```bash
cd source/
npm install          # installs better-sqlite3 (used by db.aggregate pattern)
node --test l16.test.mjs   # run all 8 tests — should all pass (green)
node l16.red.mjs           # demonstrate the red state (exit 1)
```

## Key result

```
✔ T1: calling rev() before registration raises no such function (1.39ms)
✔ T2: scalar UDF rev() registered via db.function reverses a string
✔ T3: nat_key UDF enables natural sort ordering via ORDER BY
✔ T4: custom aggregate sum_sq([1,2,3]) = 14  (1²+2²+3²)
✔ T5: custom aggregate sum_sq returns start value (0) for empty group
✔ T6: custom aggregate sum_sq([7]) = 49
✔ T7: BINARY collation orders item2 after item10 (proves the problem)
✔ T8: nat_key UDF ordering places item2 before item10 (proves the fix)
tests 8 · pass 8 · fail 0
```

## Driver

- `node:sqlite` (Node 24.15.0, SQLite 3.51.3) — scalar UDF + custom aggregate
- `better-sqlite3` 12.11.1 installed (confirms same aggregate API shape; aggregate is exercised via node:sqlite in this POC)

# Intent — L16: Custom Functions, Collations, and Aggregates

## POC level
L16

## POC name
Custom Functions, Collations, and Aggregates

## Concept introduced
Extend SQL from the host language: register a scalar UDF (`db.function`), a custom aggregate (`db.aggregate` with `{start, step, result}`), and demonstrate ordering equivalents of custom collations via scalar UDFs — all callable from SQL.

## Prior concepts reused
- L1 (CRUD): `CREATE TABLE`, `INSERT`, `SELECT` with parameterized queries
- L9 (aggregation context): SQL aggregate functions over row groups; the custom aggregate replaces a built-in aggregate

## Live service boundary exercised
SQLite 3.51.3 engine called via `node:sqlite` (Node 24.15.0 built-in). No network, no external process. The "service" is the embedded SQLite engine.

## Real resources required
None. SQLite is keyless and local. `better-sqlite3` npm package for confirming the aggregate API shape across drivers.

## Expected learning
After this POC the learner can:
1. Register any JS function as a SQL scalar UDF callable from all SQLite statements.
2. Implement stateful aggregates in JS with the `{start, step, result}` protocol.
3. Understand that BINARY (lexicographic) collation misorders strings with embedded numbers, and fix it with a nat_key UDF.
4. Understand the driver gap: neither `node:sqlite` nor `better-sqlite3` exposes `sqlite3_create_collation`, so true `COLLATE <name>` clauses require a C extension.

## What this POC must prove
1. Calling `rev()` from SQL before registration raises `"no such function: rev"`.
2. After `db.function('rev', fn)`, `SELECT rev('abc')` returns `'cba'`.
3. `db.aggregate('sum_sq', {start:0, step, result})` computes sum-of-squares correctly for multi-row, empty, and single-row groups.
4. BINARY collation puts `item10` before `item2` (wrong natural order).
5. `nat_key()` UDF in `ORDER BY` puts `item2` before `item10` (correct natural order).

## What would count as cheating
- Mocking the SQLite call or pre-computing the result in JS before passing to SQLite.
- Using a fake output file instead of a real terminal run.
- Claiming `COLLATE <custom>` works without proving it live.

## Why cheating would destroy the learning
The entire value of this POC is knowing exactly which APIs exist at runtime. If the collation claim is fabricated, the learner will spend time debugging a `no such collation sequence` error in production. The driver gap (no `createCollation`) is a real gotcha that must be documented honestly.

Task from the nextjs curriculum (POC level L2-server-vs-client-components):

# L2: The 'use client' Boundary — Server Shell + Interactive Client Island

## What This Proves

This POC demonstrates the server/client boundary in Next.js 16's App Router:

1. **Server Component** (`app/widget/[id]/page.tsx`): An `async` Server Component that:
   - Does server-only work (`process.version` — Node version, never exposed to the browser)
   - Awaits async `params` (Next 16 requires `await params` — params is a `Promise`)
   - Embeds a `'use client'` island (`<Counter start={Number(id)} />`) seeded with a server-computed prop

2. **Client Component** (`app/components/Counter.tsx`): Marked `'use client'` (line 1), uses `useState` and `onClick` for interactivity. Server-renders its initial HTML, then hydrates in the browser.

3. **Client Page** (`app/client-widget/[id]/page.tsx`): A `'use client'` page component that is NOT async but reads the `params` Promise via React's `use()` hook.

4. **Dynamic Routes (`ƒ`)**: Both `/widget/[id]` and `/client-widget/[id]` have no `generateStaticParams`, so they render on demand (Dynamic `ƒ` marker in build output).

## Key Boundary Rules Proven

- `'use client'` must be the FIRST line of the file (not a comment above it)
- Server Components CAN import Client Components (server → client is allowed)
- Client Components CANNOT be `async` — use React `use()` to read Promises
- `params` is a `Promise` in Next 16 — always `await` it in Server Components, use `use()` in Client Components
- The server-rendered HTML contains the client island's initial markup BEFORE hydration

## Red → Green

**RED 1 (useState in Server Component):** Writing `import { useState } from 'react'; useState(0)` in an async server component (no `'use client'` directive) causes:
```
You're importing a module that depends on `useState` into a React Server Component module.
This API is only available in Client Components. To fix, mark the file (or its parent) with the "use client" directive.
```
Build exits 1. No route table.

**RED 2 (Regression — sync params access):** Typing params as `Promise<{id: string}>` but accessing `.id` synchronously:
```
Type error: Property 'id' does not exist on type 'Promise<{ id: string; }>'.
```
TypeScript phase fails; build exits 1. Locks in the async-params contract.

**GREEN:** Move `useState` to the `'use client'` Counter island; `await params` in the server component → build succeeds, all assertions pass.

## How to Run

Prerequisites: Node 24

```bash
npm install
npx next build
npx next start -p 3003
# then in another terminal:
curl localhost:3003/widget/7
curl localhost:3003/client-widget/42
```

## Files

- `app/components/Counter.tsx` — `'use client'` island with `useState`/`onClick`
- `app/widget/[id]/page.tsx` — async Server Component that awaits params, does server work, embeds Counter
- `app/client-widget/[id]/page.tsx` — `'use client'` page reading params via React `use()`

Task from the pydantic-ai curriculum (POC level L0-validated-output):

# L0 — Validated Output (Type-Safe Agent Contracts)

## What this proves

A Pydantic AI `Agent` configured with `output_type=ReviewSummary` enforces a **runtime
type contract** on the LLM's output. Against the **real** OpenAI API (`gpt-4o-mini`), the
agent's result is a validated `ReviewSummary` instance — `sentiment` is constrained to the
`Literal['positive','negative','neutral']` enum and `key_points` is a non-empty `list[str]`.
No mocks: every test issues a live provider request.

This is the core "type-safe agent contract" claim: the agent's output is schema-checked at
runtime, not merely parsed as free text.

## What's in here

- `source/review_contract.py` — the `ReviewSummary` Pydantic model + `build_agent()`.
- `source/test_review_contract.py` — RED, GREEN, and regression tests (all live).
- `red-output.txt` / `green-output.txt` — raw pytest output proving red→green.
- `evidence.md` — captured live-service evidence (validated dict, model id, usage counts).
- `surprises.md` — gotchas discovered while building.

# L4 — Marker Variants and Render Prop

Evidence: Live verified

## What This POC Proves

The `Marker` compound component renders the chat meta-row contract (date separators, system notes, stream-status banners) on real DOM. `Marker` uses `@base-ui/react useRender` so its host element is polymorphic: the default is a `<div>`, or any caller-supplied element via the `render` prop (e.g. a `<button>` for a tappable tool-activity row). Both `data-slot="marker"` and `data-variant=<variant>` are emitted via `useRender`'s `state` object and survive a render-prop tag swap. `MarkerIcon` is a decorative `aria-hidden` span; `MarkerContent` carries the accessible label.

## Real Boundary Crossed

RTL + jsdom — real React 19 reconciler on real DOM. Crosses the real `@base-ui/react useRender` boundary: the `render={<button type="button"/>}` case actually produces a `<button>` DOM node in jsdom, with `data-slot` and `data-variant` set by the primitive's `state` injection, and `onClick` merged via `mergeProps`.

## How to Run

```bash
# From the degree harness app root (_poc-app/)
cd shadcn/degrees/01-chat-components/_poc-app && npx vitest run poc/L4-marker-variants-and-render-prop.test.tsx
```

Expected: 5 tests, 5 pass, exit 0. Runtime ~456 ms.

## Prerequisites and Credentials

None. The harness requires only:

- `npm ci` (or `npm install`) to restore `node_modules`
- The installed `src/components/ui/marker.tsx` (written by `npx shadcn@latest add marker --yes` during L0)

No API keys, no network calls at test time.

## Ports and Cleanup

No ports opened. No network traffic during the test run. No cleanup required.

## Red→Green Summary

RED: A test asserted that a default `<Marker>` (no variant prop) has no `data-variant` attribute. It does — `variant` defaults to `"default"` in the cva call (L24), so `data-variant="default"` is always present on every rendered `Marker`. The assertion `.not.toHaveAttribute("data-variant")` fails with `Received: data-variant="default"`.

GREEN: Assertions corrected — `data-variant` is always present; the three variant class sets are assertable as className substrings; the render-prop swaps the host to a real `<button>` with `data-slot`/`data-variant` surviving; `MarkerIcon` carries hardcoded `aria-hidden="true"`. 5 tests pass.

# POC Intent

POC level: L4
POC name: Marker Variants and Render Prop
Concept introduced: `Marker` as the chat meta-row component (date separators, system notes, stream-status banners); three variants (`default`, `separator`, `border`) with their cva class sets; `Marker`'s `useRender` polymorphism (default `<div>`; any element via `render` prop); `MarkerIcon` as a decorative `aria-hidden` span; `MarkerContent` as the accessible label span.
Prior concepts reused: `useRender` render-prop polymorphism (L2–L3); cva `defaultVariants` (L2–L3); `data-slot` / `data-variant` contract (L1–L3); RTL + jsdom harness (L1–L3).
Live service boundary exercised: RTL + jsdom (real React 19 reconciler + real `@base-ui/react useRender`). No network at test time.
Real resources required: Installed `src/components/ui/marker.tsx` (written by L0 CLI install, single file, no `registryDependencies`). No API keys.
Expected learning: `variant` defaults to `"default"` in the cva call, so `data-variant` is ALWAYS present — asserting its absence is a false RED. The `markerVariants` cva call merges `className` into the cva call itself (`markerVariants({ variant, className })`, marker.tsx L32) rather than composing with `cn()` afterward — assert on substring presence, not exact equality. `data-slot`/`data-variant` on `Marker` come from `useRender`'s `state` object (not literal JSX attributes), so they survive the render-prop tag swap. `MarkerIcon.aria-hidden` is hardcoded `"true"` — accessibility targets must use `MarkerContent`, not `MarkerIcon`. `marker` has no `registryDependencies` but still imports `@base-ui/react` + cva, which only exist because the base-nova preset init installed them.
What this POC must prove: (1) Default `<Marker>` renders as `<div>` with `data-slot="marker"` and `data-variant="default"`. (2) `variant="separator"` adds `before:h-px`, `after:h-px`, `before:flex-1` to className. (3) `variant="border"` adds `border-b`, `border-border`, `pb-2` to className, and `group/marker` base token is still present. (4) `render={<button type="button"/>}` → real `<button>` with `data-slot="marker"` + `data-variant="border"` surviving and `onClick` firing. (5) `MarkerIcon` is a `<span>` with `data-slot="marker-icon"` and hardcoded `aria-hidden="true"`; `MarkerContent` has `data-slot="marker-content"`.
What would count as cheating: Mocking `@base-ui/react`; asserting computed CSS (Tailwind not evaluated in jsdom); importing from a local fixture; asserting exact `className` string equality (cva class order is not guaranteed).
